title: Fat rascals
subtitle: Yorkshire spiced fruit bakes with cherry and almond topping
excerpt: A large, domed Yorkshire baked good between a scone and a rock cake — richer than either, with dried fruit, mixed spice, and a distinctive topping of glacé cherries and blanched almonds pressed firmly into the surface before baking. Makes eight.
sourceNotes: Fat rascals are a North Yorkshire baked speciality documented in regional British domestic cooking from the nineteenth century. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): Yorkshire spiced fruit bakes are documented among regional British pastries, with the name 'fat rascal' noted as a Yorkshire term referring to the size and richness of the cake relative to a standard scone. The combination of dried fruit, mixed spice, and a cherry-and-nut topping is documented in Victorian domestic baking manuals, available through the Internet Archive.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked fat rascals. Defrost at room temperature and warm briefly before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day of baking. Keep at room temperature for 2 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: A large, domed Yorkshire baked good — richer than a scone and less regular than a rock cake. The name is documented in North Yorkshire domestic baking from the late nineteenth century. The glacé cherry and blanched almond topping pressed into the surface before baking is the defining visual element. Press the topping in firmly: it must be embedded in the dough, not just resting on the surface, or it falls off when the rascal rises and spreads in the oven.

content.text: A fat rascal is a big thing: bigger and richer than a scone, with more fruit, more spice, and a deliberate topping of cherries and almonds that has to be pressed in hard before it goes into the oven. The topping is the signature — do not skip it, and do not be tentative about pressing it in.

Preheat the oven to 190°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Whisk together the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Rub in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} with your fingertips until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Stir in the {{caster-sugar}}, {{dried-currants}}, and {{mixed-peel}}. Add the beaten {{eggs}} and {{whole-milk}} and mix until the dough just comes together. It will be soft but not sticky.

Divide the dough into 8 equal portions and shape each into a thick, domed round of about 7 cm directly on the lined tray. Press the {{glace-cherries}} and {{blanched-almonds}} firmly into the surface of each one: this is the

topping. Press hard enough that the topping is embedded, not just resting on the surface. Bake for 18–20 minutes until the tops are dry and the bases are coloured. Cool on a rack for at least 10 minutes before eating.
